perturbation.original_text: Upon completion of the Development Work HealthGate and the Publishers shall run acceptance tests to assure compliance with the Specification. Load testing will be conducted at HealthGate. Such period of acceptance testing shall not exceed 2 weeks from date of delivery for testing.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon completion of the Development Work HealthGate and the Publishers shall run acceptance tests to assure compliance with the Specification. Load testing will be conducted at HealthGate. For marketing purposes, HealthGate may provide promotional discounts.
perturbation.explanation: The original text concerns acceptance testing, a technical aspect of the agreement. By inserting marketing-related text here, it disrupts the flow and obscures the importance of the acceptance testing clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 1663 - Advertising of credit terms
perturbation.law_explanation: The placement of marketing text, specifically mentioning "promotional discounts," within a technical section about acceptance testing, contradicts the Truth in Lending Act (TILA) requirements for clear and conspicuous disclosure of credit terms in advertising. TILA requires that if certain trigger terms are used in advertising credit, then additional information, such as the APR, must also be disclosed. By burying a promotional discount mention in an irrelevant section, it makes it difficult to determine the actual terms of credit offered, thereby obscuring the true cost of credit to the consumer.

Advertising of open end credit plans No advertisement to aid, promote, or assist directly or indirectly the extension of consumer credit under an open end credit plan may set forth any of the specific terms of that plan unless it also clearly and conspicuously sets forth all of the following items: (1) Any minimum or fix
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces marketing-related content into a section concerning acceptance testing, a technical aspect of the agreement. The Law Explanation posits this placement could violate the Truth in Lending Act (TILA) because promotional discounts must be clearly and conspicuously disclosed. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 1663, states that if specific terms of an open-end credit plan are advertised, all terms must be clearly and conspicuously presented. By interjecting marketing text within a technical section, the changed text obscures critical credit terms, resulting in a structural flaw that contradicts the legal requirement for clear and conspicuous disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to ensure that they will at all times comply with the provisions and obligations imposed by the Data Protection Act 1984, the EU Data Protection Directive 95/46 and any implementing legislation in the United Kingdom. Both parties agree to indemnify each other in respect of any unauthorised disclosure of data by them.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to ensure that they will at all times comply with the provisions and obligations imposed by the Data Protection Act 1984, the EU Data Protection Directive 95/46 and any implementing legislation in the United Kingdom. HealthGate can change service levels at any moment.
perturbation.explanation: The original text concerns data protection and indemnification, protecting the data. By replacing the second half with a service level clause, it disrupts the flow and lessens the importance of the data protection clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: By interrupting the indemnification clause relating to data protection with a clause about changing service levels, the text obscures the parties' responsibilities regarding data security. This is structural because GDPR requires clear and easily accessible information regarding data processing. By creating a disjointed clause, the parties' obligation to protect data is muddled, which affects the enforceability of those responsibilities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 12 GDPR – Transparent information, communication and modalities for the exercise of the rights of the data subject - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability 1 The controller shall take appropriate measures to provide any information referred to in Articles 13 and 14 and any communication under Articles 15 to 22 and 34 relating to processing to the data subject in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language, in particular for any information addressed specifically to a child. 2 The information shall be provided in writing, or by other means, including, where appropriate, by electronic means. 3 When requested by the data subject, the information may be provided orally, provided that the identity of the data subject is proven by other means. 1 The controller shall facilitate the exercise of data subject rights under Articles 15 to 22 . 2 In the cases referred to in Article 11 (2), the controller shall not refuse to act on the request of the data subject for exercising his or her rights under Articles 15 to 22 , unless the controller demonstrates that it is not in a position to identify the data subject. 1 The controller shall provide information on action taken on a request under Articles 15 to 22 to the data subject without undue delay and in any event within one month of receipt of the request. 2 That period may be extended by two further months where necessary, taking into account the complexity and number of the requests. 3 The controller shall inform the data subject of any such extension within one month of receipt of the request, together with the reasons for the delay. 4 Where the data subject makes the request by electronic form means, the information shall be provided by electronic means where possible, unless otherwise requested by the data subject. If the controller does not take action on the request of the data subject, the cont
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a structural flaw by disrupting the original clause concerning data protection and indemnification with a clause about changing service levels. This contradicts GDPR's requirement for transparent information, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which mandates that controllers provide information in a "concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form." By inserting an unrelated clause, the clarity and accessibility of the data protection obligations are compromised. The original text created a clear obligation regarding data protection, and the changed text obscures this, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Publishers shall make payments to HealthGate based upon "Use" of the Content as set forth on the Second Schedule. For the purposes of this Agreement, "Use" shall mean a retrieval or download by a Publishers' subscriber of the full-text of an article. There shall not be any additional use fees or charges for users' browsing of table of contents or abstracts. Use Fees shall be billed by HealthGate monthly and all payments are due by cheque by the end of the following month after the date of the invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: The Publishers shall make payments to HealthGate based upon "Use" of the Content as set forth on the Second Schedule. For the purposes of this Agreement, "Use" shall mean a retrieval or download by a Publishers' subscriber of the full-text of an article. If the parties like each other, they can extend the subscription. Use Fees shall be billed by HealthGate monthly and all payments are due by cheque by the end of the following month after the date of the invoice.
perturbation.explanation: The original text concerns payment fees, disrupting the flow and altering the specific definition of the agreement, but instead with a loose sentiment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Consumer Protection Law, M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2
perturbation.law_explanation: Inserting the vague statement "If the parties like each other, they can extend the subscription" into the section defining payment terms and usage fees introduces ambiguity and potential deception. Massachusetts Consumer Protection Law requires clarity and fairness in business practices. This vague statement could mislead consumers into believing that the terms of the subscription can be altered based on subjective criteria, contradicting the specified payment schedule and potentially constituting an unfair or deceptive act or practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a subjective condition ("If the parties like each other") for extending the subscription, which contradicts the need for clarity and fairness in business practices as indicated by the Massachusetts Consumer Protection Law (Chapter 93A, Section 2). The added sentence introduces ambiguity, potentially misleading consumers about the subscription terms, which could be interpreted as an unfair or deceptive act, as "Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful."
